summary: Alfred is a single-threaded, single-process TUI application. There are no threads, no async/await, no Arc/Mutex, and no network I/O. The concurrency surface is entirely confined to: (1) Rc<RefCell<EditorState>> shared between the event loop and Lisp bridge closures -- which is safe within a single thread but susceptible to runtime borrow panics; (2) a single AtomicU64 for buffer ID generation using Ordering::Relaxed; (3) hook dispatch that carefully extracts callback Rc pointers before releasing the borrow to allow callbacks to re-borrow state. No race conditions, no N+1 database patterns, and no async issues were detected. The primary risk is RefCell double-borrow panics at runtime in edge cases.

thread_safety_issues.description: NEXT_BUFFER_ID uses Ordering::Relaxed for the atomic fetch_add. In a single-threaded context this is correct. If Alfred ever becomes multi-threaded (e.g., background syntax highlighting), this should be upgraded to Ordering::SeqCst or AcqRel to ensure visibility ordering.
thread_safety_issues.evidence: static NEXT_BUFFER_ID: AtomicU64 = AtomicU64::new(1); ... NEXT_BUFFER_ID.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed)

race_conditions.description: load_user_config checks config_path.exists() and then evaluates it. There is a TOCTOU window between the existence check and the file read. In practice this is benign for a local config file, but if the file is deleted between the check and eval_file() the error will be handled by eval_file returning an IoError.

memory_leak_risks.description: The undo_stack Vec<UndoSnapshot> has no size limit. Each push_undo stores a clone of the Buffer (Rope handle + metadata). While Rope cloning is O(1) via structural sharing, the Rope handle itself accumulates references. For large files edited thousands of times in a single session, this grows unboundedly. The change_list and jump_list are capped (CHANGE_LIST_MAX_SIZE=100, JUMP_LIST_MAX_SIZE), but undo_stack is not.
memory_leak_risks.evidence: pub undo_stack: Vec<UndoSnapshot>, ... state.undo_stack.push(UndoSnapshot { buffer: state.buffer.clone(), cursor: state.cursor, });

memory_leak_risks.description: line_styles (HashMap<usize, Vec<(usize, usize, ThemeColor)>>) and line_backgrounds grow as plugins add styles. While the renderer clears visible-range styles on each frame for syntax highlighting, plugin-generated styles (e.g., rainbow-csv) are not cleared between renders unless the plugin explicitly calls (clear-line-styles). For very large buffers where plugins apply styles to every line, this map can grow large.
memory_leak_risks.evidence: pub line_styles: HashMap<usize, Vec<(usize, usize, crate::theme::ThemeColor)>>, pub line_backgrounds: HashMap<usize, (crate::theme::ThemeColor, crate::theme::ThemeColor)>,

memory_leak_risks.description: Each registered Lisp command via define-command captures a clone of the callback Value and the Lisp environment Rc. These closures are stored in the CommandRegistry for the lifetime of the editor session. Plugins that register many commands will accumulate closure captures that are never released during the session.

resource_leaks.description: std::fs::read_to_string is used for file loading -- this opens, reads, and closes the file handle atomically, so no handle leak. Similarly std::fs::write at line 251 is atomic. No persistent file handles are kept open.

refcell_risks.description: The event loop carefully drops the RefCell borrow before executing deferred actions (DeferredAction). The comment '// borrow dropped here' marks the scope exit. This pattern is correct but brittle: any refactoring that moves code inside the borrow scope would cause a runtime borrow panic. The deferred action pattern was specifically introduced to avoid double-borrows, which implies they have been hit in the past.
refcell_risks.evidence: let (deferred, repeat) = { let mut state = state_rc.borrow_mut(); ... }; // borrow dropped here

refcell_risks.description: Hook dispatch in register_dispatch_hook carefully extracts callback Rc pointers, drops the borrow, then calls callbacks. This is the correct pattern. However, it relies on all callbacks being well-behaved and not themselves triggering a re-entrant dispatch-hook call that re-borrows state.
refcell_risks.evidence: let callbacks: Vec<Rc<hook::HookCallbackFn>> = { let editor = state.borrow(); hook::get_callbacks(&editor.hooks, &hook_name) }; let results: Vec<Vec<String>> = callbacks.iter().map(|cb| cb(&string_args)).collect();

recommendations.title: Cap undo_stack to prevent unbounded memory growth
recommendations.description: Add a MAX_UNDO_STACK_SIZE constant (suggested: 1000) to editor_state.rs and enforce it in push_undo, using the same drain pattern as change_list: if state.undo_stack.len() > MAX_UNDO_STACK_SIZE { state.undo_stack.drain(0..1); }. This matches the already-established pattern for change_list and jump_list.

recommendations.title: Add tests for RefCell double-borrow scenarios
recommendations.description: The deferred action pattern exists because double-borrows have been encountered. Add integration tests that exercise the paths most likely to cause double borrows: hook callbacks that call (dispatch-hook ...) recursively, and Lisp commands that evaluate further Lisp expressions. This guards against regressions if the borrow scope is accidentally widened.

recommendations.title: Document the single-threaded execution model explicitly
recommendations.description: The use of Rc<RefCell<>> (rather than Arc<Mutex<>>) is a deliberate design choice for a single-threaded application. Add a doc comment in main.rs or lib.rs noting that the application is single-threaded by design and that Rc<RefCell<EditorState>> is intentionally used for performance without synchronization overhead.

recommendations.title: Consider upgrading AtomicU64 ordering for future-proofing
recommendations.description: NEXT_BUFFER_ID.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed) is correct for the current single-threaded model but will become incorrect if background threads are ever added (e.g., async syntax highlighting). Upgrade to Ordering::SeqCst proactively, or add a comment documenting the assumption.
